author: Athanasius of Alexandria
interpretation: Athanasius reads 'firstborn of all creation' not as 'first created being' (the Arian interpretation) but as 'sovereign over all creation.' The immediately following clause — 'for by him all things were created' — proves the Son is Creator, not creature. If all things were created through him, he cannot himself be among the 'all things.' The word 'firstborn' denotes rank and preeminence, not temporal origin.
context: The Arian controversy turned significantly on Colossians 1:15. Athanasius's contextual argument — that the passage as a whole makes the Son Creator, not creature — became the definitive orthodox reading.

author: John Chrysostom
interpretation: Chrysostom reads the passage as a hymn of cosmic supremacy. Christ is 'the image of the invisible God' — not a copy but the exact representation, as a king's image on a coin bears his authority. All things hold together in him, meaning the universe's coherence depends on his sustaining power. Chrysostom emphasizes that this same cosmic Lord reconciles 'through the blood of his cross' — infinite power expressed in ultimate vulnerability.
context: Chrysostom's contrast between cosmic power and cruciform reconciliation captures the passage's dramatic tension. His homilies on Colossians were preached during a period of political instability in Constantinople, giving the theme of Christ's sovereignty particular urgency.

author: Thomas Aquinas
interpretation: Aquinas divides the hymn into two movements: Christ's role in creation (vv. 15–17) and his role in redemption (vv. 18–20). In creation, Christ is the exemplary cause — all things are made according to the pattern he embodies. In redemption, he is the efficient and final cause — reconciliation is accomplished through his blood and directed toward the restoration of all things to their intended order under God.
context: Aquinas's causal analysis brought philosophical precision to the hymn's poetic language. His framework influenced how systematic theologians organize the doctrines of creation and salvation as a unified divine plan.

author: John Calvin
interpretation: Calvin reads the passage as a polemic against the Colossian heresy, which elevated angelic intermediaries between God and humanity. Paul's response is total: Christ created all things including the angelic powers, he sustains all things, and he reconciles all things. There is no room for any intermediary because Christ occupies every mediatorial role. Calvin applies this against the Catholic cult of saints and angels.
context: Calvin's anti-mediator reading connected the first-century Colossian situation directly to Reformation-era debates about the invocation of saints. His commentary on Colossians was one of his earliest and most polemically charged.

author: Ernst Käsemann
interpretation: Käsemann identifies the passage as a pre-Pauline baptismal hymn originally celebrating Christ's cosmic lordship over the spiritual powers. Paul (or a Pauline editor) added the phrase 'through the blood of his cross' to anchor the cosmic hymn in the historical crucifixion. Without this addition, the hymn could tend toward a triumphalistic cosmology disconnected from the cross.
context: Käsemann's redaction-critical analysis — identifying a pre-existing hymn modified by Pauline theology — became the standard critical approach to Colossians 1:15–20. The theory remains influential even among scholars who question specific details.

author: N.T. Wright
interpretation: Wright reads the hymn as a Jewish monotheistic poem that includes Jesus within the identity of the Creator God of Genesis 1. The structure mirrors the Shema: there is one God, and Jesus is included in that one divine identity as the agent of creation and redemption. The hymn is not borrowing from Hellenistic philosophy but drawing on Jewish Wisdom traditions, now personalized in Christ.
context: Wright's reading of the Colossians hymn through the lens of Jewish monotheism rather than Hellenistic cosmology shifted the interpretive landscape. It connected Paul's Christology to its Jewish roots rather than treating it as a Hellenistic import.
